coredata.dc:title: A self-adaptive multi-color fluorescent pH probe with the ability of whole cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Zhuang, Qi
Si, Xiaojing
Li, Li
Zeng, Hongyan
Ding, Yaping
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent pH probes are promising for both in vitro and in vivo pH detections in chemical and biochemical systems. Previously, the multi-color and whole cell pH sensing is a challenge for conventional fluorescent nanomaterials. In this work, we report an N, S co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs)-based fluorescent pH probe that can response to different incident light with tunable wavelength. The emission wavelength is tunable and correlated to the excitation wavelength, enabling self-adaptive multi-color sensing. The N, S-CDs was synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method utilizing glucose, ammonium persulfate and ethylenediamine as precursors. The fluorescence of N, S-CDs shows a good linear relationship against pH values from 3.0 to 10.0 with a linear correlation coefficient of 0.996. The good biocompatibility and small size fulfill the demand of whole cell intracellular imaging. We have demonstrated that the N, S-CDs have successfully applied in HepG2 cells for the self-adaptive multi-color imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N
S-carbon dots
Fluorescence pH probe
Multi-color
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: pH measurements with N, S-Carbon dots
Characteristics of fluorescence N, S-carbon dots
The optical properties of N, S-carbon dots
The response of N, S-carbon dots to pH values

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Nature Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (No. 21671132 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A) and the diameter distribution (B) of N, S-CDs.
(A) FT-IR spectra of glucose, (NH4)2S2O6 and N, S-CDs. (B)XRD pattern of the N, S-CDs. (C) EDS pattern of N, S-CDs.

(A) Fluorescence excitation, emission and UV–vis absorption spectra of N, S-CDs; (B) Excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra of the N, S-CDs. (C) Corresponding normalized spectra. (D) Upconverted PL properties of N, S-CDs. PL emission spectra for the N, S-CDs dispersed in water at excitation wavelengths from 700 nm to 900 nm.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of N, S-CDs in 0.01 M K2HPO4/KH2PO4 buffer at various pH values. (B) A linear relationship between the fluorescent intensity and pH (3.0–10.0) of N, S-CDs. (C) pH reversibility study of N, S-CDs between pH 3.0 and 10.0. (D) UV–Vis spectra of N, S-CDs dispersed in solutions with pH 3.0 and 9.0.

(A) Effects of ionic strength on the fluorescent intensity of N, S-CDs. (B) the fluorescent intensity of N, S-CDs under the irradiation of UV light for 0–1 h. (C) Fluorescence responses of the N, S-CDs at 442 nm (excitation wavelength, 367 nm, emission wavelength, 442 nm, pH, 7.0, BR buffer) with different interfering molecules and irons (100 μM).
Cell viability for HepG2 cells in the presence of different concentrations of N, S-CDs.
Fluorescent images of cultured HepG2 cells cultivated with N, S-CDs excited under different emissions (A, B, C) by inverted fluorescence microscope.

Confocal fluorescence images of 0.5 mg/mL N, S-CDs incubating with HePG2 cells at different pH (5.0,7.0,9.0). Fluorescence images of the N, S-CDs were collected in the range of 405–630 nm, from left to right: fluorescent image excited with a 405 nm laser (green), 543 nm (red), 603 nm (yellow pseudocolor), brightfield, and overlap of the corresponding bright field image and the fluorescence image, respectively). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Comparison of other published carbon dots for pH sensing.

head.ce:title: A self-adaptive multi-color fluorescent pH probe with the ability of whole cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Public Health, Shanghai Aurora Vocation College, Shanghai 201908, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent pH probes are promising for both in vitro and in vivo pH detections in chemical and biochemical systems. Previously, the multi-color and whole cell pH sensing is a challenge for conventional fluorescent nanomaterials. In this work, we report an N, S co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs)-based fluorescent pH probe that can response to different incident light with tunable wavelength. The emission wavelength is tunable and correlated to the excitation wavelength, enabling self-adaptive multi-color sensing. The N, S-CDs was synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method utilizing glucose, ammonium persulfate and ethylenediamine as precursors. The fluorescence of N, S-CDs shows a good linear relationship against pH values from 3.0 to 10.0 with a linear correlation coefficient of 0.996. The good biocompatibility and small size fulfill the demand of whole cell intracellular imaging. We have demonstrated that the N, S-CDs have successfully applied in HepG2 cells for the self-adaptive multi-color imaging.

Schematic illustrations for the preparation N, S-CDs and their Application for Intracellular pH Sensing, multi-color cell imaging.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Real-time monitoring environmental pH is of great importance in both living and man-made systems. As a key parameter for chemical reactions, slight pH change may break chemical equilibrium, bring byproducts, and influence final yield. Besides, intracellular pH plays a pivotal role in the function of cell activation, cellular metabolism, growth and proliferation [1–4]. Abnormal intracellular pH in living cells may lead to physiological dysfunction even diseases [5]. Decreasing intracellular pH may induce apoptosis and produce acidic organelles during endocytosis and digestion [6,7]. Many diseases, such as Alzheimer's disease [8], cancer [9] and stroke [10], are all found to be caused by destruction of intracellular pH balance.

Among many techniques [11–13], fluorescent probes have attracted increasing attention for pH measurements due to the remarkable sensitivity, selectivity, rapidity, applicability and low cost [14,15]. Various nanomaterials as fluorescent pH probes have been reported, including organic dyes [16], silicon nanodots [17], metallic nanoclusters [18], polymer nanoparticles (NPs) [19] and quantum dots [20]. However, the excitation and emission wavelength of some materials are strictly restricted in a specific area, posing a limit on the development of multi-color fluorescent sensors. Besides, most of these probes cannot achieve the whole cell intracellular imaging owing to their large size or low biocompatibility.

Recently, carbon dots (CDs) [21] have attracted considerable attention for the applications of chemical and biochemical sensors, due to their stable fluorescence, less toxicity and good biocompatibility [22–24]. For many CDs, the emission wavelength is tunable and correlated to the excitation wavelength, enabling self-adaptive multi-color sensing [27]. The small size feature of CDs may facilitate the diffusion into living cell organelles for whole cell imaging [25] and drug delivering [26]. Further, the doping of other elements may lead to fluorescence enhancement and/or surface functionalization for integrating with other objects.

Herein, towards the circumventing of restrictions in multi-color and whole cell pH detections, we report an N, S co-doped CDs-based fluorescent pH probe that can response to different incident light with tunable wavelength. The N, S co-doped CDs (N, S-CDs) was synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method that using glucose as the C source, ammonium persulfate as the S source and ethylenediamine as the N source. The obtained N, S-CDs exhibits tunable excitation wavelength, low toxicity, good biocompatibility and up-conversion fluorescence feature. Especially, the N, S-CDs exhibit pH-dependence in the pH range of 3.0–10.0, which makes them as good pH probes for biosensor applications.

Ammonium persulfate, ethylenediamine and K2HPO4, KH2PO4, KOH were received from Sinopharm Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Glucose (Glu), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), 4-Hydroxyethylpiperazine ethanesulfonic acid (HEPES) were acquired from Aladdin Chemistry Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazo-lium bromide) were received from keyGEN BioTECH (Nanjing, China). Quinine sulfate, H3PO3 were obtained from Shanghai Maclean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. The ultrapure water was used for all testing experiments and all chemicals were of analytical reagent grade that could be used without further purification.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) of N, S-CDs was performed on an JEOL-200CX transmission electron microscope (Japan). UV–vis spectra of the carbon dots were received on an UV-2501PC spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were received on the AVATAR 370 (America), X-ray diffraction (XRD) were performed on 3KW D/MAX2200V (Japan). The fluorescence spectroscopy of CDs was obtained on an RF-5301PC spectrofluorophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). All fluorescent images of the cells were performed by confocal laser scanning microscopy (Zeiss LSM710, Germany) and inverted fluorescence microscope (DM14000 B, Germany).

In this work, the N, S-carbon dots were prepared according to a literature method [28]and we made some changes in the synthesis steps. First, 0.5 g ammonium persulfate, 0.5 g glucose, and 1.0 mL ethylenediamine mixed under stirring. The mixture was stirred into a yellow viscous material and then added 10 mL deionized water to afford a clear yellow solution. Then the mixture was placed in the 30 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 2 h and then cooled to room temperature in air. After the reaction, the color of the mixture turned dark. Next, the N, S-carbon dots were collected by further purification, dialysis with pure water for 24 h (change water every 8 h) using a dialysis bag (MWCO:1000). Finally, the purified N, S-carbon dots were uniformly dispersed in pure water and low temperature stored at 4 °C for further characterization and experimentation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH measurements with N, S-Carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The pH detection of N, S-CDs were in 0.01 M phosphate buffered solution (PBS) of varied pH values (pH 3.0–10.0) at room temperature. First, 100 μL N, S-carbon dots aqueous solution was added into set of dry 25 mL calibrate flasks with different pH (3.0–10.0) 0.01 M PBS. Second, transferring the resulting solution into 1cm quartz cells for fluorescence detection. The fluorescence emission spectra of N, S-CDs were recorded at room temperature excited at 367 nm and emitted between 377 nm and 680 nm.

Cytotoxicity test of carbon dots should be performed before cell imaging. Hence, the cytotoxicity of N, S-CDs was measured by the MTT colorimetry [29] First, 100 μL HepG2 cells (density = 1 × 104 cells/mL) were added to each well of a 96-well plate and cultured in a 37 °C, 5% CO2 cell incubator for 24 h. Then 5 μL various concentrations of test compound N, S-CDs stock solution were added to every well and then cultured for another 24 h. Afterwards, MTT were subjected to a 5-fold dilution and then added 50 μL diluted MTT to each well. Then the HepG2 cells were incubated at 37 °C for 4 h to restore MTT to formazan. Next, Aspirated the supernatant in each well and added 150 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) solution to each well to dissolve formazan following by shaking with plate shaker for 15 min, and then the absorbance of MTT at 490 nm was measured by an automatic Microplate reader (TC10, singapore). The calculation of cell viability is as the following formula:
T/C
(%)
=
(
OD
treated
−
OD
blank
)
/
(
OD
control
−
OD
blank
)
where, T/C is cell viability, T is the OD value of the treated cells, and C is the OD value of the control cells. ODtreated and ODcontrol are the absorbance of cell in the presence and absence of N, S-CDs, respectively. ODblank is the absorbance of MTT with no cells.

HepG2 cells were treated with N, S-carbon dots (0.5 mg/mL) for 6 h in a 37 °C, 5% CO2 cell incubator. Then the cells were washed with pH 7.4 PBS buffer for three times in order to remove the residual cell culture medium and carbon dots attached to cell surface. Then the pH7.4 buffer solution was then removed and replaced with the high-K+ HEPES-buffer solution with different pH 5.0, 7.0, 9.0 from acid to base and incubated for 15 min, respectively. The multi-color fluorescence images of HepG2 cell were performed by confocal fluorescence microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of fluorescence N, S-carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and structure of N, S-carbon dots were represented by several characterization methods. As illustrated in Fig. 1 A, N, S-CDs exhibited nearly spherical structure and well dispersed in ethanol medium. The diameters of N, S-carbon dots were centred at 4–10 nm in Fig. 1B, which was in line with the proper size of the carbon dots.

The FTIR spectra were used to characterize relevant the functional groups of N, S-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 A, a broad absorption band at 3065-3504 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of OH bond and NH bond. An absorption band at 2896-2964cm−1 is the characteristic absorption of CH. And the absorption peak at 2104cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of CN. Another obvious FTIR bands correspond to the CO bond at 1646 cm−1. In addition, the stretching vibrations of SO3 are located at 1077 cm−1 [28]. These bonds indicate that the CDs are successfully doped with N, S elements. The EDS analysis of N,S-CDs were performed in Fig. 2C, it showed some atomic peaks corresponding to N, S, C and O of N, S-CDs and the elemental analysis demonstrated the composition of the carbon dots was C 53.77 wt%, S 4.60 wt%, N 13.21 wt%, and O 28.43 wt%. Besides, Fig. 2B shows the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of N, S-CDs, the sharp peak located in the 22.8° [30], which was assigned to the [002] plane that demonstrates the graphitic structure of N, S-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optical properties of N, S-carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the optical properties of the as-prepared N, S co-doped carbon dots, the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were both considered. As shown in Fig. 3 A, there is an absorption peak centred at 343 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum, which is attributed to n-π* transition of CO bond. The fluorescence excitation wavelength (λex) of N, S-CDs was 367 nm and the max emission wavelength (λem) was 442 nm (Fig. 3A). Besides, as illustrated in Fig. 3B, the N, S-carbon dots showed excellent excitation wavelength-dependent feature that the position and intensity emission wavelength depend on their excitation wavelength. This feature allows carbon dots to emit multi-color under different excitation wavelengths, which is suitable for their bio-imaging applications. And as shown in Fig. 3BC, when the fluorescence excitation wavelength increased from 367 nm to 470 nm, the N, S-carbon dots’ fluorescence emission position was red-shifted from 440 nm to 550 nm because of their excitation wavelength-dependent feature and have a gradual decrease in the photoluminescence intensity.

Meanwhile, the relative quantum yield (QYu) of N,S-CDs was measured as 10.34%, which used quinine sulfate (QYs = 54%, in 0.1 mol-1 H2SO4, λex = 360 nm) as a standard by comparing the integrated fluorescence area F and Uv–vis absorbance values (A = 0.01–0.1) at 360 nm of the CDs aqueous solutions and the quinine sulfate standard solution, and the detailed calculation is shown in the following formula: QYu = QYs(Fu Asnu 2)/(FsAuns 2), where u is the sample solution and s is standard [31].

Interesting, the N, S-CDs were shown to posse up-conversion properties [32], which means the carbon dots emit high-energy light when excited by low-energy light. As illustrated in Fig. 3D. When the N, S-carbon dots were excited with low-energy excitation (Near IR-light) in the range of 700–900 nm, the PL spectra showed an emission peak at 442 nm and red shift as the excitation wavelength varies, the emission was obtained in the range of 440–500 nm with good PL intensity which was similar to their corresponding down-conversion excitation-dependent PL behaviors, clearly supporting the up-conversion PL characteristic of prepared N, S-CDs. Therefore, the as-prepared N, S-CDs may have potential applications in bioimaging and biosensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The response of N, S-carbon dots to pH values
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence intensity of N, S-carbon dots was notably affected by pH values. We measured the fluorescence emission spectra of the carbon dots in 0.01 M K2HPO4/KH2PO4 buffer solution at different pH values of 3.0–10.0. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity of N, S-carbon dots was observed to be an obvious decrease as the pH increased from 3.0 to 10.0. The N, S-CDs displayed a good linear relationship Y = −62.58X+875.62 in the range of pH 3.0–10.0 with a linear correlation coefficient of 0.996 was shown in Fig. 4B. What's more, the fluorescence probe based on N, S-CDs posse a good reversibility between pH 3.0 and 10.0 (Fig. 4C). To further investigate the influence of pH changes on the other optical properties of N, S-CDs, the UV absorption spectra at different pH was performed. We detected the Uv–vis absorbance of N, S-CDs at pH 3.0 and pH 9.0 at a wavelength of 200–500 nm and result showed that the absorption peak at pH 3.0 is larger than that of pH 9.0 in the band of 220–400 nm in Fig. 4D. As the pH changes from acidic to alkaline, both the fluorescence intensity and Uv–vis absorption peak of N, S-carbon dots have changed, and the mechanism of pH-dependent fluorescence probe may be that the deprotonation of the surface group of the N, S-CDs [33]. What’s more, other pH detection methods by carbon dots were also compared with our novel N, S-CDs in Table 1 , it is apparent that the N, S-CDs in this work has a more reasonable detection range than most other methods.

Excellent stability of N, S-CDs is much significant for their application in biological sensors. Herein, we considered the influences of the irradiation time and ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of N, S co-doped carbon dots. To research the influence of ionic concentration on the fluorescence stability of the carbon dots, it can be seen that almost no obvious change in the fluorescence intensity of as-prepared CDs was observed at a KCl concentration of high up to 2.0 mol/L (Fig. 5 A), which demonstrating that CDs exhibited high stability in high ionic strength. What's more, we also explored the effect of ultraviolet radiation on the fluorescence stability of that CDs. No obvious decrease of fluorescence intensity was observed under continuous UV irradiation of 60 min (Fig. 5B), indicating the good photostability of N, S-CDs. Besides, we also measured the effect of potentially interfering substances on N, S-carbon dots. As shown in Fig. 5C, these interfering substances include various cations (K+, Zn2+, Na+, Mg2+), amino acids (histidine, tyrosine, leucine, glycine, phenylalanine, valine, cysteine) and glucose. Upon addition those interferences respectively, the concentrations of these interferences cations and amino acids are 100 μΜ, and the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs after the addition of interfering substances were also surveyed. It can be seen that the influence of these species over their physiological concentrations (100 μΜ) on the fluorescent intensity of carbon dots was negligible that demonstrating that the carbon dots showed excellent selectivity towards pH sensor. Therefore, the N, S-CDs have high photoluminescence stability and selectivity is a promising fluorescence nanomaterial for sensing applications.

In order to explore the potential application of N, S-CDs in fluorescence imaging of living cells, MTT assays were performed to investigate the cytotoxicity of N, S-CDs. As shown in Fig. 6 , there was no obvious decrease in cell viability was noticed after HepG2 cells were treated with N, S-carbon dots at a high concentration (up to 0.6 mg/mL) for 24 h, the cell viability is high to 95%, clearly demonstrating that obtained CDs have low toxicity and good biocompatibility in living cells. Therefore, the above conclusions showed that the N, S-CDs have lower toxicity and perfect biocompatibility that are fit for pH fluorescence imaging in living cells.

Based on the low cytotoxicity and of excellent optical properties the N, S-CDs, we have studied its application of fluorescence imaging in living cells. First, 0.5 mg/mL concentration of N, S-CDs stock solution were added to HepG2 cells and then cultured for another 24 h. Then washed off the residual carbon dots on the surface of cells with PBS 7.4 and imaging with fluorescence microscope. As shown in Fig. 7 , owing to N, S-CDs’ small size and their good feature of emitting fluorescence with different color under excitation of different wavelengths of light, the N, S-CDs could easily enter into HepG2 cells and exhibit multi-color photoluminescence under the fluorescence microscope. This multicolor fluorescent phenomenon of that carbon dots was attributed to the characteristics of excitation wavelength-dependent property and was well reflected in cell imaging.

Furthermore, we have explored the multicolor imaging and pH-dependent behavior of N, S-CDs and now we combine both two excellent features for further experimental exploration. Using confocal fluorescence microscopy to multi-color imaging of HepG2 cell which incubated with N, S-CDs at three different pH values. The fluorescence images of HepG2 cultured with N, S-CDs were collected under 405 nm, 543 nm, 603 nm excitation. Fig. 8 showed the confocal fluorescence microscopy images of the HepG2 cells after the endocytosis of N, S-CDs at different pH values (5.0, 7.0, 9.0). The fluorescent of carbon dots was present in both the cell nucleus and cytoplasmic area of the cell, demonstrating that the N, S-carbon dots could easily penetrate into the whole cell due to their small size. Furthermore, the N, S-CDs-incubated HepG2 cells displayed strong multi-color fluorescence emission at pH 5.0 (channel 1 (blue, λex = 405 nm), channel 2 (red, λex = 543 nm), channel 3 (yellow pseudocolor, λex = 603 nm). With the pH increasing from 5.0 to 9.0, the fluorescent brightness of N, S-CDs in cells was gradually decreased under different excitation. The brightness changes of N, S-CDs in living cells demonstrates that the pH of the intracellular solution has changed. The results indicates that fluorescent pH probe depended on N, S-CDs could easily penetrate in living cells and simultaneously self-adaptive multicolor imaging to sense the pH change and could be used as fluorescence platform to monitor pH fluctuations in living cells [34,35].

In summary, we have successfully developed N, S co-doped CDs-based fluorescent pH probe by hydrothermal treatment. The N, S-CDs were found to exhibit excellent optical properties, a tunable wavelength, low cytotoxicity and even up-conversion properties. Importantly, the obtained CDs demonstrated the pH-dependent luminescence behavior, which was behaved as fluorescence intensity decrease with the increase of pH in the range of pH 3.0–10.0 with a linear correlation coefficient of 0.996. Meanwhile, the N, S-CDs can response to different incident light with tunable wavelength. Benefiting from the prepared CDs’ good biocompatibility and small size, they have successfully applied in HepG2 cells for the self-adaptive multi-color imaging.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (No. 21671132).
